comment_text:
“We are not horses. We are human. Humans are..."
Gi-hun couldn’t finish that sentence—not because he didn’t know what to say, but because the truth about humanity is too heavy to put into words. Humanity is complicated. Cruel. Beautiful. Broken. It can't be explained in a single sentence. When he said, “We are not horses,” he was calling out how the VIPs treated the players as entertainment, as animals to gamble on. Just like horses used in wars and races, the players were pushed to their limits, used, discarded. That’s the entire point of Squid Game: humans being dehumanized for the sake of profit and power. And the baby who won? That wasn’t just a coincidence. Someone always has to sacrifice for someone else to live. Even if it's a child who survives, it’s because someone good gave everything. Squid Game shows that in this world, good people often suffer while bad people wait for karma to arrive if it ever does. All the players were poor. But they weren’t just chasing money they were chasing hope, a second chance, a way out. The game was built to reveal something deeper the raw truth about humanity. In the end, Gi-hun didn’t need to finish that sentence because the entire story of Squid Game already did.
Is that true, Netflix?

The hormones these people take really damages them and gives them long term health problems and irreversible damage.   He can do whatever he wants, but just because you want to be something doesn't mean it'll magically happen.   There are people who really want to be cats but they can't ever be.   What they need is to be reminded how special they are and that they can't escape in a dreamworld forever

comment_text:
Honestly... I didn’t vibe with Season 2 and 3 at all. It went from intense survival mode to soft, emotional drama real quick. All the strong characters die early, and we’re left with a pregnant lady, a baby, and an old woman?? Like what even...
It got frustrating and every time a strong one tried to help, they died. And the characters felt so flat or just plain annoying (girl with black magic? glad she’s gone tbh). The hero turning psycho felt super forced too. He didn’t even react this much when he killed his own brother. 
This season felt like a watered-down version. I miss the raw, brutal energy!!

comment_text:
Could barely breathe through all the crying, and I couldn’t be happier to have been able to experience this story. 

 No piece of media has ever touched me this deeply, and it has left me with such a profound feeling of hope I never would have believed could come from a show like this.

Thank you for decorating my time space with such impactful, poignant, and special energy. I will deeply miss seeing this story unfold, but am happy these characters can be free from this hell. 

We are not horses. 😭

Can’t wait to see what everyone involved with this does next! What a phenomenal cast and crew to be able to put together something this good!!

comment_text:
안녕하세요, 이정재 님.

저는 러시아에서 온 팬입니다. ‘오징어 게임’ 시즌 3를 보고 너무 큰 감정을 느껴서 이렇게 편지를 쓰게 되었습니다.

마지막에 기훈이 말한 "우리는 말이 아니야, 우리는 사람이야"라는 대사는 제 마음을 깊이 울렸습니다. 그 순간, 저는 눈물을 참을 수 없었습니다. 이 말이 저에게는 단순한 대사가 아니라, 인간의 고통과 존엄성에 대한 절규처럼 느껴졌습니다.

기훈이라는 캐릭터를 통해 인간성과 희망, 고통과 용기에 대해 다시 생각하게 되었습니다. 그 연기에 진심으로 감사드립니다. 이정재 님의 연기는 저의 마음을 울렸고, 오랫동안 기억에 남을 것입니다.

혹시 이 편지가 닿지 않더라도, 진심으로 감사하다는 말을 전하고 싶었습니다.
항상 건강하시고, 앞으로도 멋진 작품으로 많은 사람들에게 감동을 주시길 바랍니다.

감사합니다.

– 당신의 팬, [Твоё имя или просто “러시아 팬”]

video_title: Squid Game: Season 3 | Final Games Trailer | Netflix
comment_text:
I think people misunderstood the ending of Squid Game Season 3. For me, it wasn’t about whether the main character survives — it was about the message.

The story shows how people behave when pushed to their limits, especially for money. In Season 3, there’s a scene where people start arguing about killing a newborn baby just to increase their chances of winning. That reflects what actually happens in the real world — greed, selfishness, and how people stop caring about others. The baby wasn’t even a week old, but money turned everyone against her. This is exactly the kind of human nature the Front Man used to believe in.

But then Gi-hun comes in and proves something different — that even in a place full of death and greed, someone can still choose to care about others, even if they’re not related. His sacrifice at the end isn’t just emotional; it’s a complete rejection of the system. He dies so the baby can live — and that alone destroys the message the Games were trying to send.

He says, “We are not horses. We’re humans. But humans are…” and even though the line isn’t finished, the meaning is clear — humans are complex. Some are cruel, but some still have empathy. That’s why I think it’s a good ending. Not from the point of view of the main character’s survival, but from the moral side — the message that not everyone becomes a monster in hard times.

Now about the bigger picture — I think Squid Game isn’t just a Korean thing. In Season 3, when the Front Man reveals his face to Gi-hun, he says “If I die, they’ll replace me.” That means there’s a system above him. He’s not the highest authority — just a branch manager.

And in the final episode, Cate Blanchett’s character appears in Los Angeles and sees the Korean Front Man — and she recognizes him. That proves this is a global operation. It’s like a franchise, just like how banks in India have branches in every city. Squid Game has different branches in different countries, and each one has a Front Man running it. The one we saw in Korea was just one of many.

So the island in Korea was one branch. The U.S. scene at the end shows there’s another branch. And Cate’s character is likely one of the recruiters or higher-ups who know what’s going on across all branches. That’s why she didn’t need an introduction — she already knew who the Korean Front Man was.

In short, the story isn’t about “who won the Game.” It’s about what kind of person you choose to be — even when the world treats you like an animal. Gi-hun didn’t survive, but he proved that humanity can still exist inside the Game.

That’s why I liked the ending. Not because it was happy — but because it was honest.

comment_text:
Both were right — 456 and the Frontman. The arguments of both were valid. The Frontman proved that people really are selfish, cruel, and garbage. But 456 proved that not everyone is like that — that even those who end up in a place and a game like this have a reason, a story to tell, and that many of them deserve a second chance.

But neither 456 nor the Frontman changed anything. The games ended in Korea, but they continued in the USA and the rest of the world. And people will remain the same — they will vote for another game every time, until the very end.

I understand both of them, but non of them changed single shit in this world.
